text: The surgeon begins the hysterectomy procedure by cutting the left round ligament using a harmonic scalpel, detaching the lateral attachment of the large uterus with multiple visible myomas of varying sizes.
The surgeon cuts the mesosalpines and performs a salpingectomy, ensuring both ovaries are spared during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully cuts the fallopian tube at a distance from the ovarian pedicle to preserve the ovarian vascularization.
The surgeon uses a Salp injector to cut very close to the fallopian tube, demonstrating the simplicity of the instrument in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to coagulate the adnexal pedicle, ensuring hemostatic control before progressively cutting through the pedicle.
The surgeon releases the left ovary laterally, detaching it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon begins the hysterectomy in a virgin patient by approaching the lateral aspect of the uterus, ensuring a clear view of both uterine pedicles.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the opposite side, using bipolar coagulation to ensure hemostatic control before cutting the adnexal pedicle, maintaining distance from the ovarian pedicle to preserve vascularization.
The surgeon cuts the right round ligament and the fallopian tube, then controls the utero-ovarian pedicle close to the uterus to minimize the risk of ureteral injury.
The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to precisely coagulate the tissue at a level of two centimeters, ensuring hemostatic control before cutting in the middle for a dry dissection.
The surgeon increases uterine mobility and proceeds to cut the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, moving toward the upper part of the uterosacral ligament.

The surgeon carefully avoids damaging the clearly visible uterine vein while cutting the peritoneum. The dissection of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament begins, with the large uterine vein visible below the harmonic scalpel. The vesico-vaginal peritoneum is tracked during the procedure.
The surgeon cuts the peritoneum in a dry procedure, with increased uterine mobility providing a clear view of the vaginal area.

The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine pedicle, ensuring that bipolar energy is applied directly to the vessel rather than the surrounding peritoneum. Coagulation is performed over a three-centimeter area at the isthmic region.
The surgeon refrains from cutting the pedicle at this step to manage the backflow on the opposite side, maintaining the integrity of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control the large right uterine artery and vein, pulling the uterus to the left for better access. The uterus is then cut at the isthmic level using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon performs a supracervical hysterectomy without using a uterine manipulator, cutting at the supracervical area in a patient with specific anatomical conditions.
The surgeon controls the right uterine pedicle using bipolar energy and then cuts the pedicle with cold scissors, aiming to release the uterine corpus without causing hemorrhage.
The surgeon completes the supracervical hysterectomy, achieving a bloodless surgical field, with the uterus visibly separated from the cervix.
The surgeon removes the right fallopian tube to decrease the risk of residual ovarian cancer, using surgical instruments to carefully excise the tube from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon visually identifies and controls the right ureter, which is clearly visible, demonstrating ureteral rotation.
The surgeon performs an ovariopexy using a Vipril-O suture, employing the intracorporeal knotting technique to secure the ovary and reduce the risk of ovarian torsion.
The surgeon performs an ovariopexy on the opposite side, using a large needle to facilitate the procedure and locking the knot with a specific technique.
The surgeon prepares to perform morcellation on the ovary, which is visibly well-positioned following the previous steps of the procedure.
The surgeon inserts a 15mm morcellator into the left iliac fossa, with the setup at 700 drops per minute. The morcellation process is visually observed to take approximately half an hour.
